title: Degree Spectra of Relations on a Cone
overall_summary: The paper develops the study of degree spectra of invariant relations on countable structures after relativizing to a Turing cone, with the aim of isolating model-theoretic rather than presentation-dependent behavior. Its main structural results include: Harizanov’s c.e.-degree fullness theorem becomes a clean cone dichotomy; there are nevertheless incomparable d.c.e. degree spectra on every cone; and any degree spectrum on a cone not contained in the Δ^0_2 degrees contains all 2-CEA degrees. The core techniques are movable-tuple coding via free or d-free tuples, finite-injury constructions of copies with prescribed relation degree, and a game argument that maintains coding requirements while approximating a 2-CEA set. For the original automorphism problem, the paper is useful because the graph of a transported automorphism is a binary relation on copies, and the cone-fullness/definability results give routes from failure of infinitary definability of such a relation to the existence of copies where the relation has nontrivial degree, potentially contradicting computable AUT-countability on a cone.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 1.1 and Section 2.3: degree spectrum of a relation on a cone
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure and let R be an invariant relation on A, so that for every copy B isomorphic to A there is a well-defined corresponding relation R^B on B. For a Turing degree d, define dgSp(A,R)_{≤d} = {deg(R^B) ⊕ d : B is a copy of A with B ≤_T d}. Equivalently, requiring B ≡_T d gives the same set, by Knight’s upward-closure theorem for degree spectra of structures. Two relations R on A and S on B have the same degree spectrum on a cone if dgSp(A,R)_{≤d} = dgSp(B,S)_{≤d} for all degrees d in some Turing cone. More generally, containment and incomparability of these spectra are interpreted on a cone, yielding a partial order of cone-degree-spectra denoted dgSp_rel(A,R). For common relativizable classes Γ, such as c.e. degrees, d.c.e. degrees, Δ^0_α degrees, or α-CEA degrees, Γ is viewed as the map d ↦ Γ(d), and one says that dgSp_rel(A,R) contains Γ if this containment holds on a cone.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The only nontrivial equivalence in the definition is replacing B ≤_T d by B ≡_T d. If B ≤_T d is a copy of A, Knight’s theorem gives a d-computable isomorphism from B to a copy C with C ≡_T d. Transporting R along this isomorphism preserves the degree of R modulo d, so deg(R^C) ⊕ d = deg(R^B) ⊕ d. For comparing two spectra, the set of degrees on which equality, proper containment one way, proper containment the other way, or incomparability holds is Turing invariant; by Borel determinacy in the applications, one of these alternatives holds on a cone. This permits the paper to talk about a single partial ordering of spectra on a cone rather than about individual presentations.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.3: intrinsic definability on a cone
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure and R an invariant relation on A. For an ordinal α, R is intrinsically Σ^0_α on a cone if, for all degrees d in some Turing cone and every copy B of A with B ≤_T d, the relation R^B is Σ^0_α(d). The paper observes that R is intrinsically Σ^0_α on a cone if and only if R is definable in A by a Σ^in_α formula of L_{ω_1ω}, with no computability restriction on the infinitary disjunctions/conjunctions and with finitely many parameters allowed. Analogous statements hold for Π^0_α, Δ^0_α, and other standard intrinsic definability notions after relativization to a cone.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: One direction is direct after choosing a cone whose base computes the infinitary formula and the relevant parameters: evaluating a Σ^in_α formula in any d-computable copy is a Σ^0_α(d) property. For the converse, the paper invokes the standard relatively intrinsic definability theorem of Ash, Knight, Manasse, Slaman, and Chisholm, relativized high enough to compute the structure, relation, parameters, and syntactic searches. On such a cone, all effectivity conditions that appear in nonrelativized Ash-Nerode-style theorems become decidable from the base oracle, so intrinsic complexity on the cone is equivalent to infinitary definability in the underlying structure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.5: Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman and Chisholm
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a computable structure and R an invariant relation on A. For a computable ordinal α, the following are equivalent: (1) R is relatively intrinsically Σ^0_α, meaning that for every copy B of A, not necessarily computable, the transported relation R^B is Σ^0_α in the atomic diagram of B; (2) R is definable in A by a computable infinitary Σ^c_α formula with finitely many parameters from A. The analogous equivalence holds for Π^0_α and hence for Δ^0_α.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this result rather than reproving it. The easy direction evaluates a computable infinitary Σ^c_α formula in any copy B using B as oracle, giving a Σ^0_α(B) definition. The hard direction uses the standard back-and-forth/generic-copy method: if no computable infinitary Σ^c_α definition exists, one constructs a copy in which the relation fails to be Σ^0_α relative to the copy, by forcing tuples with matching lower-level back-and-forth behavior but different R-status. This theorem is the relative analogue of Ash-Nerode and is used in the paper as the bridge between formal infinitary definitions and presentation-independent computability.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2 and Corollary 2.7: Harizanov c.e.-degree fullness on a cone
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Harizanov’s theorem, in the form used by the paper, says: let A be a computable structure and R a computable relation on A that is not relatively intrinsically computable. Suppose an effectiveness condition holds: for every tuple of parameters c, one can computably find a tuple a in R such that whenever a quantifier-free formula θ(c,a,b) holds, there are a' not in R and b' with θ(c,a',b') holding. Then for every c.e. set C there is a computable copy B of A such that R^B ≡_T C. After relativizing to a cone, the effectiveness condition is absorbed into the cone base, yielding Corollary 2.7: for every structure A and invariant relation R on A, either dgSp_rel(A,R) = Δ^0_1, i.e. R has computable degree in all copies on a cone, or dgSp_rel(A,R) contains all c.e. degrees.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction underlying Harizanov’s theorem uses Ash-Nerode free tuples. If R is not relatively intrinsically computable, then over any finite tuple of parameters one can find a tuple whose current R-status can later be changed while preserving the finite atomic or existential facts already committed in the copy. To code a c.e. set C, build a computable copy B by a finite-injury construction with designated tuples b_x. Initially b_x is placed so that its R-status codes x not in C. If x enters C, the finite partial isomorphism is adjusted, using freeness, so that b_x now has the opposite R-status while preserving the finite diagram already enumerated. Injury is controlled by ordering the requirements. From R^B one can read whether each x entered C, and from C one can compute the final isomorphism, so R^B ≡_T C. On a cone, the searches for free tuples and witnesses become computable in the base oracle, so the conclusion becomes the clean dichotomy stated in Corollary 2.7.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.3 and Proposition 3.4: d-free tuples and the cone criterion for being strictly above c.e. degree
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R be an m-ary relation on a structure A. For tuples c and a from A, a is d-free over c if for every tuple b and every finitary existential formula φ(c,u,v) true of a,b, there are a',b' satisfying φ(c,u,v) such that the restriction of R to tuples from c,a' differs from the restriction of R to tuples from c,a, and such that for every finitary existential formula ψ(c,u,v) true of a',b', there are a'',b'' satisfying ψ(c,u,v) with the restriction of R to tuples from c,a'',b'' equal to the restriction of R to tuples from c,a,b. In the unary case, this says: a not in R is d-free over c if every existential neighborhood of a can be matched by some a' in R, and every later existential neighborhood of a' can be matched back by some a'' not in R while preserving the R-statuses of the auxiliary tuple b. Proposition 3.4 states: if for every tuple c there is a tuple a that is d-free over c, then the cone-degree-spectrum dgSp_rel(A,R) strictly contains the c.e. degrees. Conversely, if R is formally d.c.e. and dgSp_rel(A,R) strictly contains the c.e. degrees, then for every tuple c there must be a d-free tuple over c.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The forward direction is Proposition 3.9 relativized to a cone: d-free tuples let one build a copy in which R has degree not equal to any c.e. degree, while Harizanov’s cone theorem supplies all c.e. degrees once nontriviality is known. The reverse direction is Proposition 3.5 relativized: if R is formally d.c.e. and some tuple c has no d-free tuple over it, then the apparent difference of two c.e. definitions can be reorganized in every computable copy into a pair of c.e. sets whose join is Turing-equivalent to R^B, so R^B is always of c.e. degree. Thus, for formally d.c.e. relations, the existence of d-free tuples over all finite parameter tuples is exactly the obstruction to being intrinsically of c.e. degree on a cone.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.5: no d-free tuple implies intrinsically of c.e. degree for formally d.c.e. relations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a computable structure and R a formally d.c.e. relation on A, meaning R is definable by a formula α(x) ∧ not β(x) where α and β are computable infinitary Σ^c_1 formulas, with finitely many parameters allowed. Suppose there is a tuple c over which no tuple a is d-free. Assume effectively that, given a and c, one can find witnesses b and an existential formula φ(c,u,v) showing that a is not d-free over c, and further, given a',b' satisfying φ, one can find the existential formula ψ required in the negation of d-freeness. Then for every computable copy B of A, the transported relation R^B is of c.e. Turing degree.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is written for unary R, with the general case requiring only heavier notation. Fix c and incorporate it into the language. Since R is formally d.c.e., write R = α ∧ not β with α,β Σ^c_1. In a computable copy B, α defines a c.e. superset and β a c.e. correction set, but R^B itself need not compute those raw c.e. approximations. Using the failure of d-freeness, for each a not in R choose an existential formula φ_a(a,b_a) such that any attempt to move a into R through φ_a is followed by a formula ψ forcing any later return preserving the auxiliary R-pattern to put the main element back in R. Define a c.e. set P of elements d of B by a stage condition: d appears to satisfy α and not β at two stages s<t, and every relevant φ_a(d,e) seen by stage s has, by stage t, acquired the corresponding ψ-witness. Then R^B ⊆ P. Also P is c.e., and P minus R^B is c.e. because it is exactly the part of P satisfying β. The crucial point is that R^B computes P: if d is in R^B, then d is in P; if d is not in R^B, the no-d-free witness φ_a eventually appears with the auxiliary tuple’s true R-pattern, and this lets R^B recognize that d can never enter P. Hence R^B ≡_T P ⊕ (P minus R^B), a c.e. degree.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.9: d-free tuples build a copy whose relation is not of c.e. degree
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a computable structure and R a computable relation on A. Suppose that for every tuple c there is a tuple a not in R that is d-free over c, and suppose these d-free tuples and the witnesses in the definition of d-freeness can be found effectively. Then there is a computable copy B of A such that R^B is not of c.e. Turing degree.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a finite-injury priority construction, presented for unary R. Build a computable copy B through finite partial isomorphisms F_s from an initial segment of ω into A. Requirements R_{e,i,j} diagonalize against the possibility that R^B = Φ_i^{W_e} and W_e = Φ_j^{R^B}, where W_e ranges over c.e. sets. A requirement first chooses a fresh location n and maps it to a d-free a not in R over the current finite range. It waits for computations R^B[0..n] = Φ_i^{W_e}[0..n] with use u and W_e[0..u] = Φ_j^{R^B}[0..u] with use v. It then uses d-freeness to move the image of n to a' in R while preserving the finite diagram, thereby breaking the first computation. If W_e changes below u to repair the first computation, the second half of d-freeness supplies a'' not in R and auxiliary images preserving R^B below v, so W_e cannot still agree with Φ_j^{R^B} below u. Lower-priority requirements are injured when the partial isomorphism is changed. Standard finite-injury verification shows each requirement is eventually satisfied, so no c.e. set is Turing-equivalent to R^B.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Examples 3.13 and 3.14; Theorem 3.12 / Theorem 1.6: incomparable d.c.e. degree spectra on every cone
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There are computable structures A and M with relatively intrinsically d.c.e. relations R on A and S on M such that neither R nor S is intrinsically of c.e. degree, even relative to any cone, and the degree spectra dgSp_rel(A,R) and dgSp_rel(M,S) are incomparable on every cone. The structures are explicit tree-based multi-sorted structures. In A, one sort is the tree ω^{<ω} with child relation and root; another sort supplies 0,1,or 2 witnesses attached to each tree node. Nodes 0^n have type 0, nodes σˆa have type 1 if a is odd and type 2 if a is even, and R is the set of type-1 nodes. In M, the tree sort is ordered by the tree partial order, there is an analogous type component from a second sort, and a third sort marks most odd-ending nodes; S is again the set of nodes with first type component 1. Both R and S are relatively intrinsically d.c.e. and have d-free tuples over every finite parameter tuple.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The d-free tuples are the long zero strings 0^n above all parameters: they can be moved first to an odd successor to change R-status, and then to an even successor to return while preserving the relevant finite existential information. Thus Proposition 3.9 shows that both spectra strictly contain c.e. degrees. Incomparability is proved by two priority constructions. Proposition 3.15 builds a computable copy N of M such that no computable copy B of A has S^N ≡_T R^B. Requirements diagonalize against pairs of reductions between S^N and R^{B_e}. The construction uses two nested coding locations in N; when a computation forces B_e to mimic a change, the tree geometry of A ensures that its remaining possible d-free nodes are trapped below a node that has already changed type, so a second change in N produces an irreversible disagreement. Proposition 3.17 builds conversely a computable copy B of A such that no computable copy N of M has R^B ≡_T S^N. This direction needs an infinite-injury construction with subrequirements: if diagonalization is not immediately possible, the alleged copy N must provide infinitely many nodes outside a fixed subtree with certain low types, which is impossible in M. Both arguments relativize uniformly to any degree d, giving copies below d and showing incomparability relative to every cone.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.3 and Corollary 4.4: Σ^in_1 relations on (ω,<) are well-quasi-ordered and finitary existential
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix finitely many parameters c in the standard structure (ω,<). For each n, the set of n-ary relations on (ω,<) that are definable by Σ^in_1 formulas over c is a well-quasi-order under reverse inclusion: there is no infinite strictly increasing sequence under inclusion and no infinite antichain. Consequently, every Σ^in_1-definable relation on (ω,<) over finitely many parameters is equivalent in (ω,<) to a finitary existential formula and is computable in the standard copy, uniformly from such a finitary existential description and the parameters.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First ignore parameters and restrict attention to increasing n-tuples; the finitely many possible equality/order patterns are handled by a finite product. Any existential condition on an increasing tuple is equivalent to requiring lower bounds p_i on the finite gaps before and between tuple entries. Let D_p be the set of increasing tuples satisfying the gap bounds p. The map p ↦ D_p identifies reverse inclusion among these basic definable sets with the product order on ω^n, a well-quasi-order. A Σ^in_1 relation is a union of such D_p’s, and because finite powersets of well-quasi-orders are well-quasi-ordered, only finitely many maximal basic sets are needed. Parameters are handled by replacing a formula φ(c,y) by φ(x,y) without parameters: an antichain or increasing chain over c would induce one without parameters. Thus the infinitary disjunction collapses to a finite existential disjunction, giving computability in the standard presentation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 4.12, 4.13, and 4.14: α-c.e. relations on (ω,<) are finite-c.e. and of c.e. degree
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R be a relation on (ω,<). If R is intrinsically α-c.e. on a cone for any countable ordinal α, then R is computable in the standard copy and is intrinsically m-c.e. for some finite m. For any computable ordinal α, the following are equivalent for a relation R on (ω,<): R is computable and intrinsically α-c.e.; R is relatively intrinsically α-c.e.; and R is intrinsically α-c.e. on a cone. Moreover, if R is intrinsically m-c.e. for some finite m, then R is intrinsically of c.e. degree: in every computable copy B of (ω,<), R^B has c.e. Turing degree.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The collapse from α-c.e. to finite m-c.e. uses Lemma 4.3. A formal α-c.e. definition is a transfinite alternating union of increasing Σ^in_1 sets A_β and B_β. Since Σ^in_1-definable sets over fixed parameters are well-quasi-ordered under inclusion, the increasing sequences A_β and B_β can change only finitely many times; hence the α-c.e. definition reduces to a finite Boolean alternation of Σ^in_1 sets, each equivalent to a finitary existential formula. The equivalence of intrinsic, relative intrinsic, and cone-intrinsic α-c.e. uses Ash-Knight free-tuple criteria plus the same well-quasi-ordering to make the needed effectiveness conditions automatic for (ω,<). For intrinsic c.e.-degree, write R as a finite alternating difference of intrinsically Σ^0_1 sets A_1 ⊇ A_2 ⊇ ... ⊇ A_m. After ignoring a finite initial segment and parameters, the last nontrivial difference supplies two existential sets B ⊆ A that differ only by increasing one gap bound. From R^B one can recognize successor pairs in the copy: a carefully chosen tuple lands in A minus B exactly when a proposed y is the immediate successor of x. The successor relation is co-c.e. intrinsically and computes the isomorphism with the standard copy, so R^B is Turing-equivalent to a c.e. degree.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.19: a relation on (ω,<) with nonuniform or intermediate spectrum behavior
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There is a computable binary relation R on the standard copy of (ω,<) such that its degree spectrum on a cone strictly contains the c.e. degrees, but its degree spectrum is not uniformly equal to the Δ^0_2 degrees on a cone. Uniform equality to Δ^0_2 means that on a cone there are computable functions f,g_1,g_2 such that, from any index e for a Δ^0_2 approximation of a set C, f(e) is an index for a computable copy A_e of (ω,<) satisfying R^{A_e} = Φ_{g_1(e)}^C and C = Φ_{g_2(e)}^{R^{A_e}}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The relation R is an explicitly built directed graph on intervals [a_n,b_n), [b_n,c_n), [c_n,a_{n+1}) of ω. The intervals [a_n,b_n) and [c_n,a_{n+1}) carry matching finite cycles, there is a one-way edge connecting the starts of the cycles, and the middle interval [b_n,c_n) repeats the entire earlier pattern below a_n. This recursive self-similarity gives d-free tuples over arbitrary finite parameters, so Proposition 3.9 shows the spectrum strictly contains the c.e. degrees. To prove nonuniformity, the paper constructs, uniformly in indices e,i,j, a Δ^0_2 set C = C_{e,i,j} such that if e indexes a computable copy A of (ω,<), then either C ≠ Φ_i^{R^A} or R^A ≠ Φ_j^C. The construction of C repeatedly waits for computations in both directions, flips one bit of C to force a change in R^A below a use, and uses matching-cycle configurations in R to create links: any later repair in a farther interval forces a new element to be inserted below a fixed earlier element in A. If the reductions were correct, infinitely many elements would be forced below a single element, contradicting A ≅ (ω,<). A recursion-theorem argument then rules out any uniform functions f,g_1,g_2 realizing all Δ^0_2 degrees.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 5.5, 5.6, and Corollary 5.7: controlled tree approximation of 2-CEA sets
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let B be c.e. and A be c.e. in and above B. Lemma 5.5 gives a computable approximation f(x,s) in {0,1} such that x in A iff f(x,s)=0 for all sufficiently large s, and x not in A iff f(x,s)=1 for infinitely many s; moreover B, and hence A, can compute uniformly whether a given stage s is already permanent for f(x,t)=0 for all t≥s. Lemma 5.6 converts this into a computable approximation σ_s in T = (ω ∪ {∞})^{<ω} with a unique true path g in [T], such that a node ρ is an initial segment of g iff ρ is an initial segment of σ_s for infinitely many s, A computes g, and the binary path v(g) obtained by replacing ∞ by 0 and finite entries by 1 is A. The approximation also counts visits: if τ has length n and s_0,s_1,... are the stages with τ ⊂ σ_s, then σ_{s_0}=τ, the next coordinates start at ∞, and finite values count how many times the approximation has switched from ∞ to a finite value. Corollary 5.7 says the same holds relative to any degree d for every set A that is 2-CEA in d.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 5.5, write A = W_e^B with B_s a computable enumeration. Set f(x,s+1)=0 when x is seen in W_e^{B_s}; set it to 1 when x is not seen or when the computation witnessing x in W_e^{B_s} differs from the previous one because the B-approximation changed below the use. If the true B-computation for x in A stabilizes, f is eventually 0; if not, either x is absent infinitely often or computations change infinitely often, giving infinitely many 1s. B can decide permanence of a 0 by checking whether the finite B_s segment used in the computation is correct. Lemma 5.6 builds σ_s recursively from f by, at coordinate n, moving to ∞ when f(n,t)=0 and otherwise to a finite counter recording how often the approximation has left ∞. The construction always extends the longest previously compatible string by one new coordinate, and when it moves left of a node it never returns to that node. These properties give a unique leftmost path visited infinitely often, computable from A because A can decide permanent 0-stages.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 5.3 and Proposition 5.4: 2-free tuples and Π^0_2 nondefinability
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a computable structure C with computable relation R, a tuple a not in R is 2-free over a tuple c if for every auxiliary tuple a_1 there are a' in R and a'_1 such that c,a,a_1 ≤_1 c,a',a'_1, where ≤_1 is the first back-and-forth preorder: every finitary existential formula true of the right-hand tuple is true of the left-hand tuple. Proposition 5.4 states: if c is a tuple over which no tuple a not in R is 2-free, then the complement of R is definable by a 0'''-computable Σ^0_2 infinitary formula with parameters c. Consequently, if R is not formally Δ^0_2(0''') and, without loss of generality, R is not 0'''-computably Π^0_2-definable, then over every tuple c there is a 2-free tuple a not in R; moreover, using 0^(4) one can find such tuples.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The relation x ≤_1 y is computable by a 0''-computable Π_1 infinitary condition, because it asserts that every existential formula true of y is true of x. If no a not in R is 2-free over c, then for each a not in R there is an auxiliary a_1 such that no tuple a' in R with any a'_1 satisfies c,a,a_1 ≤_1 c,a',a'_1. Using 0''' one can choose such an a_1 uniformly. Let φ_{a,a_1}(c,x,u) be the 0''-computable Π_1 formula expressing c,a,a_1 ≤_1 c,x,u, and define ψ_a(c,x) = ∃u φ_{a,a_1}(c,x,u), a 0'''-computable Σ_2 formula true of a and false of every element of R. The disjunction over all a not in R defines the complement of R. The contrapositive supplies 2-free tuples whenever R has no formal Δ^0_2 definition at the relevant oracle level.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.1 and Corollary 5.2: fullness for 2-CEA degrees
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Theorem 5.1 states: let C be a computable structure and R a computable relation on C. Suppose R is not formally Δ^0_2(0'''), i.e. not definable by both 0'''-computable Σ^0_2 and Π^0_2 infinitary formulas with finitely many parameters. Then for every degree d ≥ 0^(ω+1) and every set A that is 2-CEA in d, there is a copy D of C with D ≡_T d such that R^D ⊕ D ≡_T A. The construction actually gives D ≤_T d, and Knight’s upward-closure theorem is used to replace D by a d-degree copy. Corollary 5.2, in cone language, says: for every structure C and invariant relation R on C, either dgSp_rel(C,R) ⊆ Δ^0_2, or dgSp_rel(C,R) contains all 2-CEA degrees.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume, after possibly replacing R by its complement, that R is not Π^0_2(0''')-definable. Proposition 5.4 supplies 2-free tuples over every finite tuple. Given A 2-CEA in d, use Corollary 5.7 to obtain a d-computable approximation σ_s through T=(ω∪{∞})^{<ω} with true path g and v(g)=A. Build D by finite partial isomorphisms F_s from initial segments of ω into C, plus a finite labeling map L_s from nodes of T to positions in D. If σ ends in ∞, L(σ) is a single coding point; if σ ends in a finite number, L(σ) labels a finite tuple with a sign vector m(σ). The construction maintains: each current approximation node has a coding location; the current F_s makes locations along σ_s have the positive coding status and immediate false extensions have the negative coding status; F_s preserves the previously enumerated atomic diagram; compatible stages agree on the corresponding initial segments; and enough elements of C enter the range for surjectivity. These conditions ensure that along the true path g the limit isomorphism F is computable from A, so A computes R^D⊕D, while R^D⊕d can recover g and hence A by checking the unique sequence of coding locations with the correct R-status. The hard part is preserving future coding flexibility. At every stage the paper maintains a closed game G_s in which Player I plays finite partial isomorphisms and Player II plays both extra elements that must be added to the range and possible future approximation nodes. I loses if the coding, atomic-agreement, or extension requirements fail. A winning strategy for I says the current construction can survive all future approximation changes without adding new coding labels. The game can be reduced to a finite basic game G^b_s: Player II need only play basic lists, because loops in the approximation can be compressed; Lemmas 5.8 and 5.9 show there are finitely many basic lists and that winning the basic game is equivalent to winning the full game. At stage s+1, first use the old winning strategy to move to the new approximation. If the new node ends in ∞, add a fresh 2-free tuple b not in R as the coding point; when a future play asks for the corresponding false finite extension, 2-freeness allows b to be switched to an R-tuple while preserving earlier commitments. If the new node ends in a finite number, enumerate the finitely many basic lists b_1,...,b_m and choose a block of coding tuples with sign vector by auxiliary games H( ... ). Lemma 5.11 says that, from a winning H for the first r lists and any candidate x, one of the two sign choices for appending x remains winning. Lemma 5.12 says that if appending x works and the tuple with x is ≤_1 the tuple with y, then appending y also works. Choose x not in R 2-free over the current range, choose y in R with the required ≤_1 relation, and use these lemmas to append a tuple whose actual R-status matches the chosen sign while preserving a winning strategy. Iterating through all basic lists gives the new coding block and the new winning strategy. This completes the construction and proves that every 2-CEA degree appears in the cone spectrum unless the spectrum is already contained in Δ^0_2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem A.1 and Corollary A.2: stronger relativization of Harizanov for the alternate spectrum
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be a computable structure and R a computable relation on A that is not intrinsically computable. Assume the following effectiveness condition: the existential diagram of the expanded structure (A,R) is computable, and given any finitary existential formula φ(c,x), one can decide whether it has finitely many or infinitely many solutions in A. Then for any sets X ≤_T Y with Y c.e. in X, there is an X-computable copy B of A such that R^B ≡_T Y, and Y computes an isomorphism between A and B. Corollary A.2 states: if A is any structure and R is a relation on A that is not intrinsically computable on a cone, then the alternate cone spectrum dgSp^*_rel(A,R), defined using dgSp^*(A,R)_{≤d} = {deg(R^B) : B ≅ A and B ≤_T d} without adjoining d to the relation degree, contains the c.e. degrees.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is designed to repair the usual relativization problem in Harizanov’s theorem: if one codes a c.e.-in-X set Y into R^B using an X-computable construction, then R^B⊕X can read the coding locations, but R^B alone may not know them. The appendix solves this by fixing all possible coding locations in advance and using an active-sequence code. Choose an infinite-to-one computable map g:ω→2^{<ω}∪{∅}. The copy B is organized into r-tuples b_i. At any time a unique increasing sequence k_1<k_2<... of active locations has R-status 'on' and g(k_1)≺g(k_2)≺...; the union of the strings is Y. Locations not on the active sequence are made permanently off or arranged never to have an active predecessor, so R^B alone can recover the unique active sequence and hence Y. The proof proceeds by induction on the arity of R and reduces to three assumptions: R only concerns tuples with no repeated entries; both R and its complement are thick, meaning they contain infinitely many pairwise disjoint tuples; and restrictions disjoint from finite parameter tuples remain non-intrinsically-computable. If R or its complement has a thick Σ^c_1 subset, the construction uses two kinds of tuples: free tuples, whose R-status can later be switched, and constrained tuples in the thick Σ^c_1 subset, which are forced to stay in R. When Y changes its current prefix, the old active free tuple is switched off and fresh constrained/free tuples are added until a new location with the current prefix is reached. In the remaining case, neither R nor its complement contains a thick Σ^c_1 subset. The appendix develops a thin/thick analysis: thin existential sets have bounded pairwise-disjoint subfamilies; if all tuples were captured by thin existential neighborhoods, one obtains a Scott family and intrinsic computability; hence there are tuples not in any thin existential set. Lemmas A.7 through A.13 show these tuples behave sufficiently like free tuples while preserving the R-status of later coding tuples. The same active-sequence construction then works. Since Y computes the enumeration changes, it computes the final isomorphism and hence R^B; conversely R^B computes Y from the active sequence.

proof_techniques.name: Relativize to a cone to remove effectivity hypotheses
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly replaces delicate computability assumptions on a structure, relation, or defining formula by working above a sufficiently high Turing degree. On such a cone, the base oracle computes the structure, relation, relevant infinitary syntax, and all searches for witnesses such as free tuples. This turns classical theorems with awkward Ash-Nerode-style effectiveness conditions into clean statements about infinitary definability and cone-degree-spectra.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a computably AUT-countable-on-a-cone structure, one can try to fix the cone base and treat transported automorphism graphs as relations whose spectra are forced to be trivial relative to that base. The paper’s cone framework supplies ready-made dichotomies once the relevant relation is shown not to have the appropriate infinitary definition.

proof_techniques.name: Free and d-free tuple coding
proof_techniques.description: A free tuple is a tuple whose relation status can be changed while preserving all finite existential information over a parameter tuple. A d-free tuple strengthens this by allowing a two-step move: first change the relation status, then later return to the original relation pattern while preserving the statuses of auxiliary elements. The paper uses free tuples for c.e. coding and d-free tuples to diagonalize against c.e. degrees or characterize when formally d.c.e. relations are intrinsically of c.e. degree.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the graph of a chosen automorphism is treated as a binary relation in an expansion by parameters, analogues of free or d-free tuples would witness that the graph can be made noncomputable in some copy. Absence of such tuples, by the paper’s arguments, often forces low-degree behavior and hence suggests a route to definability.

proof_techniques.name: Finite-injury construction by moving a partial isomorphism
proof_techniques.description: Many constructions build a computable copy B of a structure A by maintaining finite partial isomorphisms F_s from an initial segment of ω into A. The finite diagram of B is enumerated according to the current pullback. To satisfy a requirement, the construction changes F_s on a tail, using free/d-free tuples to preserve all already enumerated atomic facts while altering the transported relation on a selected coding tuple. Lower-priority requirements are injured but only finitely often.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the standard mechanism for turning lack of definability or movable tuples into a copy where a transported object, such as an automorphism graph, has a chosen degree. Solver agents can try to adapt the requirements to diagonalize against computations of automorphisms from the copy plus cone oracle.

proof_techniques.name: Coding by a unique active sequence of predetermined locations
proof_techniques.description: Appendix A fixes all potential coding locations in advance and uses an infinite-to-one map g:ω→2^{<ω}∪{∅}. The relation status of locations determines a unique increasing active sequence whose g-labels form longer and longer initial segments of the coded set. This lets R^B alone recover the coded set without needing the oracle used to build the copy, avoiding the usual problem that relativized Harizanov constructions give only R^B⊕d equivalences.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Computable AUT-countability on a cone concerns computability relative to the copy plus a fixed cone oracle, but some arguments may need relation degrees without adjoining the construction oracle. Active-sequence coding is a way to make a transported automorphism graph itself reveal the coded information.

proof_techniques.name: Game maintenance of future coding flexibility
proof_techniques.description: In the 2-CEA fullness theorem, the construction maintains not just finite coding conditions but a winning strategy in a closed game G_s. Player II represents all possible future approximation changes and all extra elements that may need to enter the partial isomorphism; Player I must respond with partial isomorphisms preserving atomic facts and coding conditions. A finite basic subgame suffices because approximation loops can be compressed. New coding blocks are chosen so that the winning strategy persists.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This technique is useful when a direct finite-injury construction cannot know future behavior of an automorphism or its graph. A solver might define a game whose winning strategies correspond to being able to keep all automorphisms computable, and then derive definability from the existence or failure of such strategies.

proof_techniques.name: Tree approximation for 2-CEA sets
proof_techniques.description: The paper represents a 2-CEA set by a computable approximation σ_s through T=(ω∪{∞})^{<ω}, with a unique true path g. The symbol ∞ represents a stable 0, while finite numbers count repeated departures from ∞. The construction’s coding locations are indexed by tree nodes; the relation status along the true path lets the final relation compute the 2-CEA set, and the set computes the true stages and hence the final isomorphism.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If attempting to show that nondefinability of automorphism graphs yields too many possible automorphism degrees, tree approximations give a controlled way to code higher-level information while preserving copy computability on a cone.

proof_techniques.name: Well-quasi-ordering of existential definitions on (ω,<)
proof_techniques.description: For relations on (ω,<), Σ^in_1 definitions collapse because existential conditions are finite gap lower bounds, and finite sets of gap bounds are well-quasi-ordered under reverse inclusion. This prevents infinite strictly increasing chains or antichains of existentially definable relations and forces transfinite α-c.e. definitions to collapse to finite alternations.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although (ω,<) itself has trivial automorphism group, the method shows how strong combinatorial restrictions on existential types can force low degree spectra. Similar WQO phenomena in a candidate structure could obstruct the desired non-Σ^in_1 automorphisms.

proof_techniques.name: Thick/thin analysis of existential sets
proof_techniques.description: Appendix A classifies existentially definable sets by whether they contain infinitely many pairwise disjoint tuples. Thin sets have a bounded number of pairwise disjoint tuples; if all tuples are captured by thin existential neighborhoods, one can build a Scott family and obtain intrinsic computability. If not, tuples outside all thin existential neighborhoods serve as robust movable tuples for coding.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For automorphism graphs, one possible obstacle is that apparent nondefinability may be localized to finitely many coordinates or parameters. Thick/thin analysis gives a way to separate genuinely abundant movable configurations from finite-dimensional artifacts.

other_useful_info: The paper emphasizes that all relations it studies are invariant relations on structures, so applying its theorems to a particular automorphism graph may require working in an expanded structure or with parameters so that the transported graph is well-defined across copies. Several open questions may matter strategically: Question 6.8 asks whether every relation has, on a cone, a maximal degree in its spectrum and whether the resulting function satisfies Martin’s conjecture; Question 6.10 asks whether degree spectra on a cone form an upper semilattice under joins; Question 6.11 asks whether the alternate spectrum dgSp^*_rel agrees above the base degree with dgSp_rel. The paper cites Ash-Nerode for intrinsic c.e. definability, Ash-Knight/Barker for higher intrinsic definability and α-c.e. relations, Harizanov for c.e.-degree spectra, and Slaman for related closure-operator fullness results.
why_relevant: Studies exactly how definability and intrinsic computability of relations behave on cones. The graph of an automorphism is a binary relation/function, so the paper’s cone-spectrum results are directly relevant to turning non-Sigma-in-1 definability into noncomputable copies or proving such a move impossible.
